risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The study describes a prospective cohort of "51 consecutive patients" and a second validation cohort recruited by neurologists, but it does not describe any allocation to intervention groups or any randomization procedure. Because no random sequence generation method is reported, there is insufficient information to judge that a truly random allocation sequence was used. Comment: Consecutive recruitment is not random allocation.

Summary: The paper states that patients were "prospectively recruited" and "included after giving their informed consent," and that the second cohort was "recruited by their neurologists," but it does not describe any mechanism for concealing the upcoming allocation or assigning participants in a way that prevented foreknowledge. This appears to be an observational prognostic cohort rather than a randomized allocation study, and no concealment method such as central allocation or opaque sealed envelopes is reported. Comment: No explicit allocation concealment procedure is described, so foreknowledge of any group assignment cannot be ruled out, but there is also no explicit open allocation method.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: This was a prognostic/predictive study with objective outcomes (EDSS change/disability progression and relapse status) rather than an intervention trial, and the key assessments were performed by trained personnel blinded to other data: "MEP studies were performed by a trained neurologist ... blinded to clinical and MRI data" and "A trained neurologist ... blinded to the MEP results, carried out the MRI analysis." Comment: Although the paper does not describe participant/personnel blinding in the classic trial sense, there is no intervention whose allocation knowledge could plausibly influence these objectively measured outcomes, so performance bias is unlikely overall.

Quote: "Neurological examination and disability assessment were performed quarterly over a 2-year period. Trained personnel scored physical disability using the expanded disability status scale (EDSS) ..." and "The patients were followed prospectively for two years by trained neurologists who obtained the EDSS at the end of the second year of follow-up." Comment: The outcome assessor was a trained neurologist/personnel who assessed EDSS, rather than patient self-report. The study does not explicitly state that the EDSS assessor was blinded to allocation, but the outcome is a clinician-rated disability scale and the review notes the outcome as objective with risk of bias from knowledge of predictors not expected. Because the outcome measurement (EDSS change) is not easily influenced by lack of blinding in this prognostic study context, detection bias is judged low.

Quote: "None of the patients refused to undergo neurological examination, MEP or MRI studies at the time of entry into the study." Comment: The paper gives no signal of post-baseline dropout affecting the main 2-year outcome analysis, and the reported test cohort still has complete analyzable outcome data for all 51 patients at follow-up in Table 1 (e.g. "N 51" and follow-up EDSS/change EDSS reported for the cohort). Reasons or numbers of loss to follow-up are not described, but the available text suggests essentially complete follow-up and no obvious imbalance or outcome-related attrition.
